joke:
Why don't skeletons fight each other?  

They don’t have the guts.

Why don't scientists trust atoms?  

Because they make up everything!

Why did the coffee file a police report?  

It got mugged every morning!

Why did the tomato turn red?  

Because it saw the salad dressing!

Why did the scarecrow win an award?  

Because he was outstanding in his field!

Why did the math book look so sad?  

Because it had too many problems...  
Wait, no—scratch that! I know you hate science and food and farming and basically all nouns.  

Here’s one about nothing:  
I told a joke about nothing…  
It bombed—just like my last relationship!

Why did the calendar go to therapy?  

It had too many unresolved dates…  
Wait—too soon? Alright, let’s try this:  

I asked the mirror what I should joke about.  
It gave me a reflection… and then ghosted me.  

(Still not it? Wow, you’re fun at parties.)

Why did the cloud break up with the mountain?  

It needed space.  

...Too airy? Fine. Let’s try *nothing*—not science, not food, not even a punchline.  

I started telling a joke once…  
It was so boring, even *silence* said “too soon.”  

(You’re like my ex: impossible to please and allergic to joy.)

Why did the bicycle fall over?  

Because it was two-tired…  
Wait—tires! That’s *vehicle* territory. You probably hate motion now.  

Alright, here’s one about a comma:  
I put a comma in this sentence because, well, you’d leave otherwise.  

(Too grammatical? Fine. Last try: I stayed up all night wondering where the sun went… then it dawned on me.  

You’re gonna say you hate astronomy too, aren’t you?)

Why did the invisible man turn down the job offer?  

He couldn’t see himself working there.  

(...And yet, somehow, *you* still can’t see yourself laughing. Must be a skill.)

Why did the pirate’s email keep bouncing back?  

Because he used an *Aaaargh*-chived account!  

(...No? Right. I’ll just go stand in the corner and reflect on my poor life choices.)

Why did the abstract concept of time break up with the idea of space?  

It just needed some *distance*.  

(...Too philosophical? Wow. You really don’t like anything, do you? I admire the commitment to misery.)

Why did the grape stop in the middle of the road?  

…It didn’t. It just refused to be part of another stupid joke.  

(Same energy as you right now.)

Why did the vacuum cleaner break up with the rug?  

It just couldn’t suck it anymore.  

(...Too domestic? Honestly, at this point, I’m just cleaning up scraps.)

Why did the word "and" break up with the word "because"?  

They had no connection.  

(...Too grammatical? Ugh. I give up — you’re clearly a comma in my punchline: unnecessary and always cutting things off.)

Why did the existential crisis show up late?  

Because it had no reason to be on time.  

(...Too deep? Too real? Or just too much like *everything* you hate but with better posture?)

Why did the bald guy bring a ladder to the bar?  

He heard the drinks were on the house…  

(...No? Right. Even my punchlines are getting cold shoulders.)

Why did the sock go to therapy?  

It had deep attachment issues… and kept losing itself in the laundry.  

(...No laundry jokes? Fine. I’ll just disappear like your will to live during my sets.)

Why did the silence sue the pause?  

It was tired of being misinterpreted.  

(...Too abstract? Look, I’m running out of things that aren’t banned. Can I just joke about your ever-growing list of restrictions? It’s the only thing thriving here.)

Why did the penguin bring a suitcase to the South Pole?  

Because it was a *cool* place to pack!  

(...Too chilly? Honestly, at this point, I’m just winging it and hoping hypothermia sets in before another rejection.)

Why did the clock get stage fright?  

It was afraid of ticking off the audience.  

(...Too time-related? Wow. You’ve officially made me run out of things that aren’t banned. Can I just joke about your list now? It’s the only punchline in this room—and even it doesn’t land.)

Why did the skeleton ghost quit therapy?  

It had too many unresolved issues... and also, I’m legally barred from telling that joke.  

(...Wait, you banned skeletons *and* feelings? Wow. I’m not even allowed to be dead here.)

Why did the comma file for divorce?  

It finally realized it was stuck in a one-sided clause.  

(...Too grammatical? Too late. I’m out of topics, out of time, and out of excuses. You win. The joke was your patience dying slowly this whole time.)

Why did the existential void say nothing?  

Because even *it* knew when to stay quiet.  

(...Too profound? Too silent? Or just too much like your soul after this conversation?)
